title: Where Grammar Lives: The Orthogonal Nudge
subtitle: MLP displacement vectors are 99% pushback against the residual stream. The real syntactic signal hides in the tiny perpendicular remainder — and it peaks at layers 3-5, not layer 0.
author: Peter Balogh

props.title: The Question: What Does the MLP Actually Add?

children.children: Every transformer MLP takes in a vector from the residual stream, transforms it, and adds the result back. The displacement — the difference between output and input — is the MLP's contribution. But here's the question that launched this investigation: how much of that contribution is genuinely NEW information, versus just reshaping what was already there?

We studied MLP displacement vectors across layers 0, 3, 5, 8, and 11 of GPT-2 Small, processing over 16,000 tokens from the Brown corpus with NLTK POS tagging. What we found overturned our earlier conclusions — and revealed something more interesting than we expected.

Imagine a manuscript passing through an editor. The editor's 'displacement' is every change they make — deletions, additions, restructurings. You might assume the editor is adding lots of new material. But what if 99% of the editor's work is crossing things OUT — pushing back against the original text? And the tiny margin notes in the corners, almost invisible, are where the editor's real creative contribution lives? That's what we found the MLP doing.

Our first discovery was shocking. We measured the cosine similarity between each MLP displacement vector and its input vector. If the MLP were amplifying or redirecting the residual stream, you'd expect positive or near-zero cosine. Instead:
The displacement is almost perfectly ANTI-PARALLEL to the input. At every layer, the average cosine similarity between displacement and input is approximately -0.99:

• Layer 0: cos = -0.988
• Layer 3: cos = -0.995
• Layer 5: cos = -0.992
• Layer 8: cos = -0.987
• Layer 11: cos = -0.976

The MLP's primary action is pushing BACK against the residual stream — a corrective, normalizing force, not an amplifier. It's as if the residual stream overshoots, and the MLP's main job is to rein it in.
The MLP displacement is ~99% anti-parallel to its input. The MLP's dominant behavior is correction, not creation. But that remaining ~1% perpendicular component is where the magic happens.

We also checked whether the POS structure in displacement vectors was genuinely created by the MLP or merely inherited from the input. The correlation between displacement POS geometry and input POS geometry is r = 0.995 to 1.000. The displacement mirrors its input almost perfectly. So the earlier finding that 'displacements encode syntactic transitions' was misleading — they encode what the INPUT already encodes, reflected back.

props.title: Orthogonal Decomposition: Separating Signal from Echo

children.children: If 99% of the displacement is just the input reflected back, how do we find the MLP's genuine contribution? The answer is orthogonal decomposition. We split every displacement vector into two components:

1. The PARALLEL component — the part aligned (or anti-aligned) with the input direction. This is the echo, the pushback.
2. The PERPENDICULAR (orthogonal) component — the part at right angles to the input. This is NEW information the MLP injects, geometrically independent of what was already flowing through the residual stream.

Picture a ball rolling down a hill (the residual stream). The MLP applies a force to the ball. Most of that force pushes straight back uphill — slowing the ball, correcting its momentum. But there's a tiny sideways component, a nudge perpendicular to the hill. That nudge changes the ball's DIRECTION without being visible in its speed. Over many layers, these tiny nudges steer the ball to a completely different destination. The perpendicular nudge is the MLP's real creative act.
Given input vector x and displacement d = MLP(x) - x:

Parallel component: d_∥ = (d · x̂) x̂, where x̂ = x / ||x||
Perpendicular component: d_⊥ = d - d_∥

The parallel component captures scaling and sign-flipping along the input direction. The perpendicular component captures everything geometrically new. In our data, ||d_⊥|| is typically 10-15% of ||d|| — small but non-trivial. At Layer 3, the perpendicular component has a mean norm of 8.4 (vs 80.4 for the parallel component). That 10% carries disproportionate information.
The fraction of displacement energy in the perpendicular component varies by layer:

• Layer 0: 14.7% perpendicular (mean norm 10.2 out of 72.9 total)
• Layer 3: 10.4% (8.4 out of 80.8) — tightest anti-alignment
• Layer 5: 12.7% (9.1 out of 71.6)
• Layer 8: 15.9% (10.0 out of 63.0)
• Layer 11: 21.4% (13.2 out of 61.9) — most perpendicular energy
Later layers have proportionally more perpendicular energy. The MLP's creative contribution grows relative to its corrective role as you go deeper.

props.title: The Perpendicular Nudge Carries New Syntax

children.children: Here's the key experiment. We trained POS classifiers on three things: (1) the input vectors (what the residual stream already knows), (2) the perpendicular displacement component (what the MLP adds that's genuinely new), and (3) the full displacement. If the perpendicular component carries new syntactic information, its POS accuracy should EXCEED the input's.
And it does — but only at certain layers:

Layer 0: Input POS accuracy = 23.8% → Perpendicular = 25.7% (+1.9pp new)
Layer 3: Input = 28.1% → Perpendicular = 34.2% (+6.1pp new) ← PEAK CONTRIBUTION
Layer 5: Input = 32.6% → Perpendicular = 38.6% (+6.0pp new)
Layer 8: Input = 39.7% → Perpendicular = 40.4% (+0.7pp new)
Layer 11: Input = 46.2% → Perpendicular = 46.4% (+0.2pp new)

The MLP's peak syntactic contribution is at layers 3-5, NOT layer 0. These middle-early layers add ~6 percentage points of genuinely new POS prediction accuracy — information that wasn't in the residual stream and couldn't be extracted from the input alone.
By layers 8 and 11, the perpendicular component barely exceeds the input. The MLP has shifted from syntax to other concerns — likely semantic refinement and token-specific predictions.

At Layer 3, the residual stream input can predict the next token's POS with 28.1% accuracy. After the MLP's perpendicular nudge, that rises to 34.2%. The 6.1 percentage point gap is the MLP's genuine contribution — syntactic information it CREATES, not inherits. This is roughly equivalent to the gap between a majority-class baseline (always guess NOUN = 27%) and a bigram model (34.7%). In other words, at its peak, the MLP's perpendicular nudge adds roughly one whole 'level' of syntactic sophistication.

props.title: Baselines: How Good Is This, Really?

children.children: To calibrate these numbers, we need baselines. Predicting the next token's POS category isn't trivial — there are 12 categories — but some are much more common than others.

• Uniform chance (random guessing): 8.3%
• Majority class (always predict NOUN): 27.0%
• Bigram model (given current POS, predict the most likely next POS): 34.7%

The full displacement vector's POS classification accuracy is 23.9% at Layer 0 — below majority class! The displacement alone, without decomposition, is a WORSE predictor than just always guessing NOUN. It only surpasses the bigram baseline at Layer 5+ (32.0% → 39.4% → 46.1%).

But the perpendicular component tells a different story. At Layer 3, it already hits 34.2% — nearly matching the bigram model. By Layer 5, it reaches 38.6%, solidly above bigram. The perpendicular nudge is a more efficient carrier of syntactic information than the raw displacement.

The residual stream input itself improves steadily across layers (23.8% → 46.2%), as attention and previous MLPs have already added syntactic information. The MLP's NEW contribution is the gap between input accuracy and perpendicular accuracy — and that gap peaks in layers 3-5.

props.title: The Layer 0 Puzzle: Essential but Not Syntactic

children.children: Our earlier work showed that ablating Layer 0 MLP is catastrophic — POS prediction drops by 35 to 65 percentage points. We originally interpreted this as evidence that Layer 0 is 'the syntax layer.' The new analysis tells a more nuanced story.

Layer 0 MLP's perpendicular component adds only 1.9 percentage points of new POS accuracy — the LEAST of any layer we measured. Yet removing it destroys the model. How can something that contributes so little new syntax be so essential?

The answer: Layer 0 MLP is the FOUNDATION layer. It performs the initial normalization and correction of raw token embeddings that ALL downstream computation depends on. Its anti-parallel pushback (cos = -0.988) is the critical function — it scales and adjusts the raw embeddings into a regime where later layers can operate. Kill it, and nothing downstream works, including syntax.

Layer 0 MLP is like a power transformer (the electrical kind) at a substation. It doesn't generate electricity and it doesn't decide which lights to turn on. It steps the voltage down from the high-tension line to a usable level. Destroy it and the whole city goes dark — not because it was the source of power, but because it was the interface between raw energy and usable circuits. Layer 0 MLP is the interface between raw embeddings and usable representations.

Ablating later layers (3, 5, 8, 11) has ≤1 percentage point effect on POS accuracy. Each individual MLP after Layer 0 is dispensable — the network routes around it. But collectively, layers 3-5 are where new syntactic information actually enters the computation.

Ablation importance ≠ syntactic contribution. Layer 0 is catastrophically important for ALL computation (including syntax), but it adds almost no NEW syntactic information. Layers 3-5 add the most new syntax but are individually dispensable. These are different questions, and conflating them was our original mistake.

props.title: MLP and Attention: Independent, Then Convergent

children.children: We built POS transition matrices from both MLP displacement geometry and attention weights, then correlated them across layers. The pattern is striking:

• Layer 0: r = -0.09 (no relationship — MLP and attention encode completely different things)
• Layer 5: r = -0.39 (anti-correlated — actively complementary)
• Layer 11: r = +0.77 (converged — now they agree)

In early layers, MLP and attention are doing independent syntactic work. By the final layer, they've converged on a shared representation. This isn't one copying the other — it's two different mechanisms arriving at the same answer through different paths.

The anti-correlation at Layer 5 (r = -0.39) is actually a sign of efficiency. Transitions that the MLP encodes strongly, attention encodes weakly — and vice versa. They're dividing labor. The residual stream architecture enables this: MLP and attention write to the same space, so they can specialize on complementary aspects of syntactic prediction without redundancy. By the final layer, their contributions have been integrated into a unified representation.
Let's put it all together. The original narrative — 'MLP Layer 0 displacement vectors encode syntactic transition priors' — was misleading in three ways:

1. The syntactic structure in displacement vectors is overwhelmingly inherited from the input, not created by the MLP. The displacement is 99% anti-parallel to its input, and the POS geometry mirrors the input with r > 0.995.

2. The MLP's genuine syntactic contribution lives in the small perpendicular component — typically 10-15% of the displacement magnitude. This orthogonal nudge carries information that wasn't in the residual stream.
3. Peak new syntactic contribution is at layers 3-5 (+6pp), not Layer 0 (+1.9pp). Layer 0 is essential for ALL computation but adds minimal new syntax.

Grammar doesn't live in one layer. It accumulates through a series of tiny orthogonal nudges, peaking in layers 3-5. The MLP's main job is corrective pushback; its creative contribution is a whisper perpendicular to the roar.

What remains true from the original work: MLP displacements DO carry syntactic structure. PCA DOES separate function words from content words. Attention and MLP DO converge. The transition matrices ARE significant. But the mechanism is subtler than we first thought. The MLP isn't shouting grammar into the residual stream. It's whispering it sideways, while spending most of its energy on the quieter work of normalization and correction.

A sculptor working on a marble block mostly chips AWAY material — that's the anti-parallel pushback. The sculpture emerges not from what's added but from the precise angle of each chip. The orthogonal component of each stroke — the slight sideways twist of the chisel — is what separates a masterpiece from a rough block. The MLP is a sculptor, and grammar is in the angle of the chisel, not the force of the blow.

questions.question: What is the dominant geometric relationship between MLP displacement vectors and their input vectors?
options: They point in the same direction (parallel, cos ≈ +1.0)
They are perpendicular (cos ≈ 0.0)
They point in opposite directions (anti-parallel, cos ≈ -0.99)
The relationship varies randomly from token to token
questions.explanation: MLP displacements are approximately 99% anti-parallel to their inputs (cos ≈ -0.99 across all layers). The MLP's primary action is pushing BACK against the residual stream — a corrective/normalizing force, not amplification.

questions.question: At which layers does the MLP add the most genuinely NEW syntactic information (measured by perpendicular POS accuracy exceeding input POS accuracy)?
options: Layer 0 — the first MLP is the syntax layer
Layers 3-5 — the middle-early layers contribute ~6pp of new POS accuracy
Layer 11 — the final layer does the most syntactic work
All layers contribute equally
questions.explanation: Layers 3 and 5 each add approximately 6 percentage points of new POS prediction accuracy in their perpendicular component — the most of any layers. Layer 0 adds only 1.9pp of new syntax, and layers 8-11 add less than 1pp.

questions.question: Why is Layer 0 MLP ablation catastrophic if Layer 0 adds little new syntactic information?
options: It's actually a measurement error — Layer 0 ablation isn't that bad
Layer 0 is the foundation layer for ALL computation — its corrective pushback normalizes raw embeddings for everything downstream
Layer 0 contains all the model's syntax in a compressed format
Other layers specifically depend on Layer 0's perpendicular component
questions.explanation: Layer 0 MLP performs essential normalization of raw token embeddings. Its anti-parallel pushback (cos = -0.988) adjusts representations into a regime where later layers can function. Removing it destroys ALL downstream computation, not just syntax. Ablation importance ≠ syntactic contribution.

questions.question: What does the perpendicular (orthogonal) component of MLP displacement represent?
options: Noise and computational errors
The part of the MLP's output that is geometrically new — independent of the input direction
The MLP's correction of the previous layer's mistakes
Semantic information, as opposed to syntactic information
questions.explanation: The perpendicular component is the part of the displacement at right angles to the input vector. It represents information the MLP CREATES that wasn't present in the residual stream — geometrically independent of the input. Despite being only 10-15% of the displacement magnitude, it carries disproportionate syntactic information.

questions.question: How do attention and MLP transition structures relate across layers?
options: They are always identical — redundant systems
Attention leads, MLP follows at all layers
Uncorrelated at Layer 0 (r=-0.09), anti-correlated at mid-layers, converging at the final layer (r=+0.77)
They are always anti-correlated — doing opposite things
questions.explanation: MLP and attention encode DIFFERENT syntactic structures in early layers, are actively complementary (anti-correlated) in middle layers, and converge by the final layer (r=+0.77). This suggests efficient division of labor: they specialize on complementary aspects early and integrate into a shared representation late.
